cards.back: An object or group of objects.
cards.explanation: A system can therefore contain one object or several objects considered together.

choices.text: An object or group of objects.
choices.feedback: The definition includes both one object and a group of objects.

choices.text: Only one object.
choices.feedback: A group of objects can also form a system.
choices.misconception: Excludes groups of objects from the definition.

choices.text: Only a group of objects.
choices.feedback: A single object can also form a system.
choices.misconception: Excludes a single object from the definition.

choices.text: A change in how energy is stored.
choices.feedback: Energy storage may change when a system changes, but the system is the object or objects.
choices.misconception: Confuses a system with a change in its energy storage.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: A system is an object or group of objects. There are changes in the way energy is stored when a system changes.

cards.front: Which three processes can change the energy involved in a system?
cards.back: Heating, work done by forces, and work done when a current flows.
cards.explanation: The two work routes are distinguished by forces and by current flow.

choices.text: Heating, work done by forces, and work done when a current flows.
choices.feedback: These are the three listed ways a system can be changed for energy calculations.

choices.text: Heating, forces acting, and work done when a current flows.
choices.feedback: Forces must do work; their mere presence is not the listed route.
choices.misconception: Treats a force acting as equivalent to work done by forces.

choices.text: Heating, work done by forces, and a current flowing without work.
choices.feedback: The listed current-flow route requires work to be done.
choices.misconception: Treats current flow alone as the specified energy-change route.

choices.text: Temperature change, work done by forces, and work done when a current flows.
choices.feedback: Heating is the listed process; temperature change is not substituted for it.
choices.misconception: Confuses a temperature change with heating as the named process.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: Throughout this section on Energy students should be able to calculate the changes in energy involved when a system is changed by: • heating • work done by forces • work done when a current flows

cards.front: Which equation calculates the kinetic energy of a moving object?
cards.explanation: At fixed mass, doubling speed makes kinetic energy four times as large.

choices.text: kinetic energy = 0.5 × mass × speed²
choices.feedback: Kinetic energy is half the mass multiplied by the square of speed.

choices.text: kinetic energy = mass × speed²
choices.feedback: The equation includes a factor of 0.5.
choices.misconception: Omits the factor of 0.5.

choices.text: kinetic energy = 0.5 × mass² × speed
choices.feedback: Speed is squared in the equation; mass is not.
choices.misconception: Squares mass instead of speed.

choices.text: kinetic energy = 0.5 × mass × speed
choices.feedback: The speed term must be squared.
choices.misconception: Treats kinetic energy as proportional to speed rather than speed squared.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: The kinetic energy of a moving object can be calculated using the equation: kinetic energy = 0.5 × mass × speed 2 Ek = 1 2 m v2 kinetic energy, Ek, in joules, J mass, m, in kilograms, kg speed, v, in metres per second, m/s

cards.front: Which equation relates elastic potential energy, spring constant and extension?
cards.back: elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant × extension²
cards.explanation: At fixed spring constant, doubling extension makes the stored energy four times as large.

choices.text: elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant × extension²
choices.feedback: The equation uses half the spring constant multiplied by extension squared.

choices.text: elastic potential energy = spring constant × extension²
choices.feedback: The equation includes a factor of 0.5.
choices.misconception: Omits the factor of 0.5.

choices.text: elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant² × extension
choices.feedback: Extension is squared in the equation; spring constant is not.
choices.misconception: Squares the spring constant instead of extension.

choices.text: elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant × extension
choices.feedback: The extension term must be squared.
choices.misconception: Treats elastic potential energy as proportional to extension rather than extension squared.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant × extension 2 Ee = 1 2 k e2 (assuming the limit of proportionality has not been exceeded) elastic potential energy, Ee, in joules, J spring constant, k, in newtons per metre, N/m extension, e, in metres, m

cards.front: Under what condition is the elastic potential energy equation assumed to apply?
cards.back: The limit of proportionality has not been exceeded.
cards.explanation: The equation is conditional on remaining within that proportional limit.

choices.text: The limit of proportionality has not been exceeded.
choices.feedback: This is the stated assumption for the equation.

choices.text: The limit of proportionality has been exceeded.
choices.feedback: The equation assumes that this limit has not been exceeded.
choices.misconception: Reverses the stated limit condition.

choices.text: Any extension is allowed, regardless of the limit.
choices.feedback: The equation is conditional on not exceeding the limit of proportionality.
choices.misconception: Ignores the equation's limit-of-proportionality condition.

choices.text: The spring constant has not been exceeded.
choices.feedback: It is the limit of proportionality that must not be exceeded, not the spring constant.
choices.misconception: Treats the spring constant as the limiting threshold.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: elastic potential energy = 0.5 × spring constant × extension 2 Ee = 1 2 k e2 (assuming the limit of proportionality has not been exceeded)

cards.front: Which equation links gravitational potential energy, mass, field strength and height?
cards.explanation: Energy is directly proportional to mass, gravitational field strength and height.

choices.text: Ep = mgh
choices.feedback: Mass, field strength and height are multiplied, with no additional factor.

choices.text: Ep = 0.5mgh
choices.feedback: The stated equation has no factor of 0.5.
choices.misconception: Adds the factor used in kinetic and elastic energy equations.

choices.text: Ep = mg²h
choices.feedback: Gravitational field strength appears once, not squared.
choices.misconception: Squares gravitational field strength.

choices.text: Ep = mgh²
choices.feedback: Height appears once, not squared.
choices.misconception: Squares height.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: Ep = m g h gravitational potential energy, Ep, in joules, J mass, m, in kilograms, kg gravitational field strength, g, in newtons per kilogram, N/kg (In any calculation the value of the gravitational field strength (g) will be given). height, h, in metres, m

cards.front: Which equation links thermal energy change, mass, specific heat capacity and temperature change?
cards.explanation: For fixed mass and specific heat capacity, a larger temperature change gives a larger energy change.

choices.text: ∆E = mc∆θ
choices.feedback: Mass, specific heat capacity and temperature change are all multiplied.

choices.text: ∆E = mc ÷ ∆θ
choices.feedback: Temperature change is multiplied, not used as a divisor.
choices.misconception: Places temperature change in the denominator.

choices.text: ∆E = m∆θ ÷ c
choices.feedback: Specific heat capacity is multiplied, not used as a divisor.
choices.misconception: Places specific heat capacity in the denominator.

choices.text: ∆E = c∆θ ÷ m
choices.feedback: Mass is multiplied, not used as a divisor.
choices.misconception: Places mass in the denominator.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: ∆ E = m c ∆ θ change in thermal energy, ∆E, in joules, J mass, m, in kilograms, kg specific heat capacity, c, in joules per kilogram per degree Celsius, J/kg °C temperature change, ∆θ, in degrees Celsius, °C

cards.front: What is the specific heat capacity of a substance?
cards.back: The energy required to raise the temperature of one kilogram of it by one degree Celsius.
cards.explanation: It specifies energy needed for a fixed temperature rise, rather than temperature rise produced by fixed energy.

choices.text: The energy required to raise the temperature of one kilogram of it by one degree Celsius.
choices.feedback: The definition fixes a one-kilogram mass and a one-degree Celsius temperature rise.

choices.text: The energy required to raise the temperature of one gram of it by one degree Celsius.
choices.feedback: The reference mass is one kilogram, not one gram.
choices.misconception: Uses a gram rather than the defined kilogram.

choices.text: The energy required to raise the temperature of one kilogram of it by ten degrees Celsius.
choices.feedback: The reference temperature rise is one degree Celsius, not ten.
choices.misconception: Uses a ten-degree rise rather than the defined one-degree rise.

choices.text: The temperature rise produced in one kilogram of it by one joule of energy.
choices.feedback: The definition gives energy required for a one-degree rise, not the rise produced by one joule.
choices.misconception: Reverses the defined relationship between energy and temperature rise.

evidence.sourceExcerpt: The specific heat capacity of a substance is the amount of energy required to raise the temperature of one kilogram of the substance by one degree Celsius.
